title: Permutation Calculator (nPr)
shortDescription: Calculate the number of ordered arrangements nPr of r items from n.
description: Compute permutations nPr = n! / (n−r)!, the number of ordered ways to arrange r items chosen from n. Enter n and r (with 0 ≤ r ≤ n) to get an instant, exact result for any combinatorics problem.
metaDescription: Free permutation calculator (nPr). Find the number of ordered arrangements of r items from n using nPr = n! / (n−r)!. Fast, exact, and easy to use.

result.label: Permutations (nPr)
result.empty: Enter n and r to see the number of permutations.

sections.heading: What is a permutation?
paragraphs: A permutation counts the number of ordered arrangements of r items selected from a set of n distinct items. Because order matters, choosing A then B is different from choosing B then A.
The formula is nPr = n! / (n−r)!. It requires 0 ≤ r ≤ n, since you cannot arrange more items than you have.

sections.heading: Permutations vs. combinations
paragraphs: Use permutations when the order of selection matters, such as ranking finishers in a race or assigning roles. Use combinations when order does not matter, like picking a committee.
Permutations always produce equal or larger counts than combinations for the same n and r, because each unordered group corresponds to several ordered arrangements.

howTo.name: Enter n
howTo.text: Type the total number of distinct items (n) into the first field.

howTo.name: Enter r
howTo.text: Type how many items you arrange (r), making sure 0 ≤ r ≤ n.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The calculator instantly shows nPr, the number of ordered arrangements.

faq.a: nPr is the number of permutations: ordered arrangements of r items chosen from n, calculated as n! / (n−r)!.

faq.q: How is nPr different from nCr?
faq.a: nPr counts ordered arrangements where order matters, while nCr counts combinations where order does not matter.
When r = n, nPr equals n!, the total number of ways to arrange all the items.
Yes. nP0 equals 1, representing the single way to arrange zero items (the empty arrangement).

faq.q: What if r is greater than n?
faq.a: That is not allowed. The formula requires 0 ≤ r ≤ n, because you cannot arrange more items than exist.
